channel.title: NYT > Top Stories
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Supreme Court Upholds Grace Period to Count Mail-In Ballots That Arrive After Election Day
item.description: The justices had been asked to examine the legality of the state’s grace period for late-arriving mail-in ballots.
item.dc:creator: Abbie VanSickle
item.media:credit: Edmund D. Fountain for The New York Times
item.media:description: A man casts an absentee ballot in Hernando, Miss., last October.

item.title: Supreme Court Rejects Trump’s Request to Appeal $5 Million Verdict in E. Jean Carroll Case
item.description: President Trump had asked the justices to intervene after a jury found that he had sexually abused and defamed the writer E. Jean Carroll.
item.dc:creator: Abbie VanSickle
item.media:credit: Sarah Blesener for The New York Times
item.media:description: In May 2023, a federal jury in New York found in President Trump liable for sexually abusing and defaming the writer E. Jean Carroll.

item.title: Israel and Iran Have Divided Democrats and Republicans. Will They Ever Be the Same?
item.description: Raging internal debates over foreign policy threaten both parties’ fortunes in November — and in 2028. Is a major ideological shift underway?
item.dc:creator: Patricia Mazzei and Anton Troianovski
item.media:credit: Angelina Katsanis for The New York Times
item.media:description: In New York’s primaries last week, two incumbent House Democrats lost to primary challengers who had cast them as insufficiently critical of Israel. Pictured are winners Claire Valdez, far left, and Darializa Avila Chevalier, far right, at a pre-primary rally with Mayor Zohran Mamdani.

item.title: Platner Has Slight Edge Over Collins in Tight Maine Senate Race, Poll Finds
item.description: The Maine race, which both parties see as key to winning control of the Senate, is extremely close as the sprint to fall begins, a Times/Portland Press Herald/Siena poll found.
item.dc:creator: Lisa Lerer, Ruth Igielnik and Taylor Robinson
item.media:credit: Edison Wu/The New York Times

item.title: Why Maine Has Turned Into Such a Tight Race
item.description: A Times/Siena poll reveals several crosscurrents in a key contest for Senate control.
item.dc:creator: Nate Cohn
item.media:credit: Sophie Park for The New York Times
item.media:description: Graham Platner’s backers are giving him a slim lead over Susan Collins.

item.title: Voters Think A.I. Is Terrible. In Campaigns, It’s Everywhere.
item.description: A.I.-generated images are the public face of this election overhaul. Behind the scenes, campaigns are using the technology to analyze voter data, craft campaign materials and write custom messages.
item.dc:creator: Stuart A. Thompson
item.media:credit: Kriston Jae Bethel for The New York Times
item.media:description: Alex Bond speaks about his political views with canvassers from Ground Truth, a door-knocking program organized by Swing Left, a progressive political group. Canvassers use an A.I.-powered app to record and analyze what voters say.

item.title: China Emerges as a Relative Winner From Strait of Hormuz Crisis
item.description: A report examines how the war in Iran has weighed on Asian economies, but left China in a more advantageous position.
item.dc:creator: Ana Swanson
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Qilai Shen for The New York Times
item.media:description: A factory of the Chinese electric carmaker Zeekr in Ningbo. China’s resources have allowed it to avoid the worst of the effects from the war in Iran.

item.title: Deep Under the Rubble, Rescuers Find an 11-Year-Old Boy Alive
item.description: A Colombian rescue team worked for six hours to recover the child, Moises, from under nearly 10 feet of rubble in La Guaira. His rescue was captured on video.
item.dc:creator: Anushka Patil
item.media:credit: Adriana Loureiro Fernandez for The New York Times

item.title: Scale of Venezuela Earthquakes Death Toll Could Take Weeks to Emerge
item.description: Experts pointed to several indications that the twin earthquakes were particularly lethal, including the number of people reported missing and the extent of building damage.
item.dc:creator: Leo Sands
item.media:credit: Adriana Loureiro Fernandez for The New York Times
item.media:description: Rescue efforts have continued for days since the devastating earthquakes in Venezuela.

item.title: Did American-Style ‘Gentle Parenting’ Spoil French Children?
item.description: As positive parenting takes over France, one psychologist’s call for a return to discipline has set off a furious debate.
item.dc:creator: Madeleine Schwartz

item.title: In San Francisco’s A.I. Era, Even $180,000 Tech Salaries Are No Longer Enough
item.description: As OpenAI and Anthropic prepare to go public, tech workers making six figures are grousing that they cannot compete with the new A.I. elite. Some doubt they can afford to stay.
item.dc:creator: Emmy Martin
item.media:credit: Christie Hemm Klok for The New York Times
item.media:description: After three months, Katrine Razniak and her partner gave up looking for a one-bedroom apartment under $5,000. “I don’t feel completely hopeless, but I don’t think I can stay in S.F.,” she said.

item.title: A.I. ‘Employees’ Might Disrupt Work in Unexpected Ways
item.description: Scholars say the “unknown unknowns” of using artificial intelligence in the workplace may be undermining the technology’s advertised benefits.
item.dc:creator: Noam Scheiber
item.media:credit: Thomas Fuchs

item.title: Comcast to Spin Off NBCUniversal, Ending a Long Union of Cable and Media
item.description: The media business, which owns NBC News and Universal Pictures, will be separated from Comcast’s cable and internet business, following a trend in the media industry.
item.dc:creator: Benjamin Mullin
item.media:credit: Lucia Vazquez for The New York Times
item.media:description: The transaction would unwind a yearslong marriage between one of the world’s largest cable and internet providers and a renowned TV and film company.

item.title: How Warsh Has Begun to Change the Fed
item.description: Just over a month into his job as chairman of the Federal Reserve, Kevin M. Warsh has embraced some conventions while signaling seismic shifts ahead.
item.dc:creator: Colby Smith
item.media:credit: Anna Rose Layden for The New York Times
item.media:description: Kevin M. Warsh, chairman of the Federal Reserve, campaigned for the job on the basis of “regime change.”

item.title: The Best Movies of 2026 So Far, and Where to Watch Them
item.description: Now is a good time to catch up on films you may have missed. Our critics have thoughts.
item.dc:creator: Stephanie Goodman
item.media:credit: Niko Tavernise/Universal Pictures and Amblin Entertainment
item.media:description: Josh O’Connor in a scene from “Disclosure Day.”

item.title: Could GLP-1 Drugs Help You Live Longer?
item.description: Experts are intrigued by the theory. Here’s what early research suggests.
item.dc:creator: Dana G. Smith
item.media:credit: Fortunate Joaquin

item.title: The Ohio City Revived by Haitian Immigration Sees an Uncertain Future
item.description: In Springfield, Ohio, some residents see the end of an economic boom after the end of a humanitarian program for immigrants. Others see still darker possibilities.
item.dc:creator: Miriam Jordan
item.media:credit: Amy Powell for The New York Times
item.media:description: Risner, who brought his family to Springfield and bought a home, fears a bleak future in Haiti.

item.title: Liberals Are Preaching Moral Values. What Took Them So Long?
item.description: Until recently, the language of morality belonged to social conservatives. But the history of liberalism suggests that values have been at its core all along.
item.dc:creator: Jennifer Szalai
item.media:credit: Matt Dorfman; Photo, via Getty Images

item.title: Naomi Osaka Serves Up a Fashion Slam at Wimbledon
item.description: The tennis player once again offered some on-court couture.
item.dc:creator: Vanessa Friedman
item.media:credit: Andrew Couldridge/Reuters
item.media:description: Naomi Osaka, the 14th-ranked women’s tennis star, took to the grass for her first Wimbledon match of the year wearing an all-white ensemble by the Japanese designer Hana Yagi.

item.title: Europe Heat Wave: Forecasts, Warnings and How to Stay Cool
item.description: A heat wave responsible for shattering numerous temperature records in Western Europe continued its shift east.
item.dc:creator: Nazaneen Ghaffar and Amelia Nierenberg
item.media:credit: The New York Times

item.title: France Recorded 1,000 Excess Deaths During Heat Wave, Officials Say
item.description: First estimates published by the national health agency listed hundreds more deaths per day compared with the daily death rate in previous months.
item.dc:creator: Ségolène Le Stradic
item.media:credit: Tom Nicholson/Reuters
item.media:description: Officials recovering the body of someone who drowned while swimming in a canal on Friday as Paris baked under a heat wave.

item.title: ‘Heat Dome’ Could Bring Triple-Digit Temperatures Across U.S. on Fourth of July Weekend
item.description: Forecasters warned that temperatures could break records in some areas. The heat is expected to last into the Fourth of July holiday weekend.
item.dc:creator: Nazaneen Ghaffar
item.media:credit: Graham Dickie/The New York Times
item.media:description: Pedestrians on the Brooklyn Bridge during a heat wave in 2024. New York City, Philadelphia and Washington are likely to have days above 100 degrees this week, forecasters said.

item.title: The Trump Vibe Shift Was Just a Mirage
item.description: The country does not love its president.
item.dc:creator: David Wallace-Wells
item.media:credit: Damon Winter/The New York Times

item.title: A.I. Is Strangling Our Economy
item.description: So much money is being poured into A.I. that the rest of the economy is starting to suffer.
item.dc:creator: Jennifer M. Harris

item.title: Migration Woes
item.description: We look into the anti-immigrant movement in South Africa.
item.dc:creator: Sam Sifton
item.media:credit: Joao Silva/The New York Times
item.media:description: Malawian nationals wait outside a migrant camp in Durban, South Africa.

item.title: For These Teens in Mexico, Soccer Is Life. Now, the Cartels Want In.
item.description: In Celaya, Mexico, local cartels have turned soccer fields into bloody battlefields as they have co-opted a local league for teenagers as their latest business endeavor.
item.dc:creator: Maria Abi-Habib and Alejandro Cegarra
item.media:credit: Alejandro Cegarra for The New York Times
item.media:description: Toros de Celaya players during a practice session in Celaya, Mexico.
